name: SQL
description: Structured Query Language for managing and querying relational databases. Used everywhere data is stored.

lessons.title: SELECT & FROM
lessons.content: SQL SELECT retrieves data from a table. The FROM clause specifies the table. Use * to select all columns or list specific column names separated by commas.

exercises.question: Which SQL clause specifies the table to query?
What does SELECT * mean?
hints: The asterisk is a wildcard meaning 'everything'

lessons.title: WHERE & Filtering
lessons.content: The WHERE clause filters rows based on conditions. Use comparison operators (=, !=, >, <, >=, <=) and logical operators (AND, OR, NOT). LIKE is used for pattern matching with % as wildcard.

exercises.question: Which SQL clause is used to filter rows?
What does WHERE name LIKE 'A%' match?

lessons.title: ORDER BY, LIMIT & OFFSET
lessons.content: ORDER BY sorts results. Use ASC (ascending, default) or DESC (descending). LIMIT restricts the number of rows returned. OFFSET skips a number of rows, useful for pagination.

exercises.question: Which keyword sorts results from highest to lowest?

lessons.title: JOIN — Combining Tables
lessons.content: JOIN combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column. INNER JOIN returns only matching rows. LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table plus matches from the right.
lessons.code_example:
-- INNER JOIN
SELECT orders.id, users.name
FROM orders
INNER JOIN users ON orders.user_id = users.id;

-- LEFT JOIN (includes users with no orders)
SELECT users.name, orders.total
FROM users
LEFT JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;

exercises.question: Which JOIN returns only rows that have matches in BOTH tables?
hints: It is the most restrictive join — both sides must match

lessons.title: GROUP BY & Aggregate Functions
lessons.content: Aggregate functions compute a value from multiple rows: COUNT(), SUM(), AVG(), MIN(), MAX(). GROUP BY groups rows sharing a value so aggregates apply per group. HAVING filters groups (like WHERE but for aggregates).
lessons.code_example:
-- Count users per country
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS total
FROM users
GROUP BY country;

-- Average order value per customer
SELECT user_id, AVG(total) AS avg_order
FROM orders
GROUP BY user_id
HAVING AVG(total) > 100;

exercises.question: Which clause filters the results of a GROUP BY?
hints: WHERE filters rows before grouping; this filters after
